content.title: 喜欢 + Noun: Expressing likes for things
content.body:
When you like a noun (person, thing, place), place the noun directly after **喜欢**:

**Subject + 喜欢 + Noun**

Examples:
- 我喜欢中国菜。(Wǒ xǐhuan Zhōngguó cài.) — I like Chinese food.
- 他喜欢北京。(Tā xǐhuan Běijīng.) — He likes Beijing.
- 她喜欢医生。(Tā xǐhuan yīshēng.) — She likes doctors.
- 我喜欢书。(Wǒ xǐhuan shū.)

content.title: Expressing immediate desires with 想
content.body:
Using **想** to express what you want to do now or in the near future:

**Stating desires:**
- 我想去北京。(Wǒ xiǎng qù Běijīng.) — I want to go to Beijing.
- 我想喝水。(Wǒ xiǎng hē shuǐ.) — I want to drink water.
- 我想吃中国菜。(Wǒ xiǎng chī Zhōngguó cài.) — I want to eat Chinese food.

**Declining or expressing lac

content.title: Negating 想 with 不想
content.body:
To say you don't want to do something, add **不** (bù) before **想**:

**Subject + 不想 + Verb (+ Object)**

Examples:
- 我不想去。(Wǒ bù xiǎng qù.) — I don't want to go.
- 他不想喝水。(Tā bù xiǎng hē shuǐ.) — He doesn't want to drink water.
- 她不想看电影。(Tā bù xiǎng kàn diànyǐng.) — She doesn't want to watch a movie.

content.title: 不要 (bùyào) - Don't / Don't want
content.body:
**不要** (bùyào) can mean "don't" (imperative) or "don't want":

As a command ("don't do something"):
- 不要去!(Bùyào qù!) — Don't go!
- 不要喝!(Bùyào hē!) — Don't drink!

As "don't want" (similar to 不想 but stronger or more definite):
- 我不要。(Wǒ bùyào.) — I don't want it.

**不想** = don't want to (softer, les

content.title: Synthesis: Preferences and desires in context
content.body:
Real-world application of 喜欢, 想, and negations:

**At a restaurant:**
- Server: 你想喝什么?
- You: 我不喜欢咖啡。我想喝茶。

**Planning with friends:**
- Friend: 你想去哪儿?
- You: 我想去看电影。你喜欢看电影吗?

**Discussing preferences:**
- New acquaintance: 你喜欢中国菜吗?
- You: 我很喜欢!我也喜欢喝中国茶。

Remember:
- Use **喜欢** for general preferenc

content.title: Making and declining suggestions
content.body:
Using 想 and 喜欢 in suggestions and responses:

**Making suggestions:**
- 你想喝茶吗?— Do you want to drink tea?
- 我们去看电影,好吗?— Let's go watch a movie, okay?

**Accepting:**
- 好!我想去。— Good! I want to go.
- 我很喜欢看电影!— I really like watching movies!

**Declining politely:**
- 对不起,我不想去。— Sorry, I don't want to

content.title: Negation patterns: 不 usage
content.body:
The negation word **不** (bù) is used consistently with 喜欢, 想, and 要:

**Pattern: 不 + Verb**

Examples:
- 喜欢 → 不喜欢 (don't like)
- 想 → 不想 (don't want to)
- 要 → 不要 (don't want / don't)

This is the standard negation pattern for most verbs in Mandarin. Simply place 不 directly before the verb.

Note: In

content.title: 想 (xiǎng) - To want to / to miss
content.body:
The verb **想** (xiǎng) primarily means "to want to" (do something) or "to think/miss."

**想** is pronounced in the third tone (falling-rising): xiǎng.

When followed by a verb, it means "to want to (do something)":
- 我想去。(Wǒ xiǎng qù.) — I want to go.
- 他想喝茶。(Tā xiǎng hē chá.) — He wants to drink te

content.title: Negating 喜欢 with 不喜欢
content.body:
To say you don't like something, add **不** (bù) before **喜欢**:

**Subject + 不喜欢 + Noun/Verb**

Examples:
- 我不喜欢猫。(Wǒ bù xǐhuan māo.) — I don't like cats.
- 他不喜欢喝咖啡。(Tā bù xǐhuan hē kāfēi.) — He doesn't like drinking coffee.
- 她不喜欢看书。(Tā bù xǐhuan kàn shū.) — She doesn't like reading books.

The stru

content.title: Comparing 喜欢, 想, and 不要
content.body:
Understanding when to use each expression:

**喜欢 (general preference):**
- 我喜欢喝咖啡。— I like drinking coffee. (in general)

**想 (current desire):**
- 我想喝咖啡。— I want to drink coffee. (right now)

**不想 (don't want to, lack of desire):**
- 我不想喝咖啡。— I don't want to drink coffee. (at this moment)

**不喜欢 (g

content.title: Difference between 喜欢 and 想
content.body:
**喜欢** and **想** express different concepts:

**喜欢** = "to like" (general preference or enjoyment)
- 我喜欢喝茶。(Wǒ xǐhuan hē chá.) — I like drinking tea. (in general)

**想** = "to want to" (specific desire at a moment)
- 我想喝茶。(Wǒ xiǎng hē chá.) — I want to drink tea. (right now)

Think of it this way:
-

content.title: Expressing preferences with 喜欢
content.body:
Using **喜欢** in natural conversations about preferences:

**Asking about likes:**
- 你喜欢什么?(Nǐ xǐhuan shénme?) — What do you like?
- 你喜欢喝茶吗?(Nǐ xǐhuan hē chá ma?) — Do you like drinking tea?

**Answering:**
- 我喜欢中国菜。(Wǒ xǐhuan Zhōngguó cài.) — I like Chinese food.
- 我喜欢看书。(Wǒ xǐhuan kàn shū.) — I lik

content.title: 喜欢 + Verb: Expressing likes for activities
content.body:
**喜欢** can also be followed by another verb to express that you like doing an activity:

**Subject + 喜欢 + Verb (+ Object)**

Examples:
- 我喜欢看电影。(Wǒ xǐhuan kàn diànyǐng.) — I like watching movies.
- 他喜欢喝茶。(Tā xǐhuan hē chá.) — He likes drinking tea.
- 她喜欢看书。(Tā xǐhuan kàn shū.) — She likes reading bo

content.title: Expressing complex preferences
content.body:
Combining 喜欢 and 想 to express nuanced preferences:

**Stating both general preferences and current desires:**
- 我喜欢喝茶,但是我现在想喝咖啡。— I like drinking tea, but now I want to drink coffee.
- 他喜欢中国菜,他想吃中国菜。— He likes Chinese food, (and) he wants to eat Chinese food.

**Expressing contrasts:**
- 我不喜欢看书,但是我想

content.title: 喜欢 (xǐhuan) - To like
content.body:
The verb **喜欢** (xǐhuan) means "to like" and is one of the most common verbs for expressing preferences in Mandarin.

**喜欢** is pronounced xǐhuan, where 喜 (xǐ) is third tone and 欢 (huan) is a light/neutral tone in standard pronunciation: xǐ·huan.

Basic structure:
- Subject + 喜欢 + object (noun or ve

content.title: 想 + Verb: Expressing desires
content.body:
Use **想** before a verb to express what you want to do:

**Subject + 想 + Verb (+ Object)**

Examples:
- 我想喝茶。(Wǒ xiǎng hē chá.) — I want to drink tea.
- 她想去北京。(Tā xiǎng qù Běijīng.) — She wants to go to Beijing.
- 他想看电影。(Tā xiǎng kàn diànyǐng.) — He wants to watch a movie.
- 我想吃饭。(Wǒ xiǎng chī fàn.)

content.title: Dialogue: Making plans
content.body:
**Context:** Two friends discussing weekend plans.

A: 你想去哪儿?(Nǐ xiǎng qù nǎr?)

B: 我想去北京。你呢?(Wǒ xiǎng qù Běijīng. Nǐ ne?)

A: 我不想去北京。我想看电影。(Wǒ bù xiǎng qù Běijīng. Wǒ xiǎng kàn diànyǐng.)

B: 你喜欢看电影吗?(Nǐ xǐhuan kàn diànyǐng ma?)

A: 我很喜欢!你呢?(Wǒ hěn xǐhuan! Nǐ ne?)

B: 我不喜欢看电影。(Wǒ bù xǐhuan kàn diàn
